Runbook auth — AUTH-001: Users being logged out / sessions invalidated unexpectedly
symptoms: Support tickets about random logouts | 401 errors spiking in gateway logs | auth-service logs show 'token not found in cache'
likely causes: Redis cluster restart or failover flushed in-memory sessions | JWT signing secret was rotated without overlap window | Redis eviction policy kicked out session keys under memory pressure | auth-service pod restarted and in-memory state was lost (shouldn't happen — check if Redis is being used correctly)
escalation: If issue persists after checking Redis and JWKS, escalate to #identity-team. Do not restart the auth-service pod without approval — it will invalidate more sessions.

Runbook auth AUTH-001 step 1: Check Redis connectivity
command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST ping
note:

Runbook auth AUTH-001 step 2: Check Redis memory and eviction
command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST info memory
note: If used_memory is near maxmemory, eviction may be culprit. Check eviction policy with CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy.

Runbook auth AUTH-001 step 3: Check recent JWT secret rotation events
command: kubectl get events -n production | grep secret
note: Look for secret rotation in the last 24h. If rotation happened, verify both old and new keys are in JWKS endpoint.

Runbook auth AUTH-001 step 4: Verify JWKS endpoint returns both keys during rotation window
command: curl -s https://auth.internal/auth/jwks | jq '.keys | length'
note: If only 1 key is returned right after a rotation, tokens signed by old key will fail. This is the most common cause. Talk to whoever rotated the secret.

Runbook auth AUTH-001 step 5: If needed, manually force re-login for affected users
command: N/A — there is no admin API for this yet. Users must log in again.
note: TODO: add session invalidation endpoint. Known gap — see JIRA IAM-441.

Runbook auth — AUTH-002: High 401 error rate (auth rejecting valid tokens)
symptoms: 401 spike visible in Datadog dashboard 'Auth Error Rate' | Logs contain 'JWT signature verification failed' or 'token expired' | Affects subset of users, not all
likely causes: Secret rotation without overlap — see AUTH-001 | Clock skew between auth-service and client (TOTP / token expiry edge cases) | Token TTL too short — check JWT_EXPIRY env var | Bug in upstream service not passing Authorization header correctly
escalation: If token kid mismatch is confirmed, this is a P1. Page the identity on-call immediately.

Runbook auth AUTH-002 step 1: Sample failing tokens from logs and decode them
command: kubectl logs deploy/auth-service | grep 'signature' | tail -20
note: Grab a sample JWT from logs and decode at jwt.io to check iat, exp, and kid fields. Do NOT share production tokens in Slack.

Runbook auth AUTH-002 step 2: Check auth-service clock drift
command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- date -u
note: Compare with your local UTC time. More than 30 seconds drift is a problem, especially for TOTP.

Runbook auth AUTH-002 step 3: Check the kid field in failing tokens against active keys
command: curl -s https://auth.internal/auth/jwks | jq '[.keys[].kid]'
note: If the kid in the failing token doesn't match any key in JWKS, the signing key has been rotated and old tokens are dead.

Runbook auth AUTH-002 step 4: Check JWT_EXPIRY and JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRY env vars
command: kubectl get deploy/auth-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env'
note: Reasonable values: JWT_EXPIRY=900 (15 min), JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRY=86400 (24h). Very short values cause frequent logouts.

Runbook auth AUTH-002 step 5: Check if a specific upstream service is not forwarding auth headers
command: Check gateway logs for requests with missing Authorization header. Usually this is a deploy misconfiguration.
note: Sometimes the issue isn't auth at all — a service just forgot to pass the token through. Check with relevant team.

Runbook auth — AUTH-003: Auth service pod OOMKilled / memory leak
symptoms: Pod restarts visible in kubectl get pods | OOMKilled exit code in pod describe | Memory grows steadily over several hours then crashes
likely causes: HTTP client connection pool not being closed after user-db or Redis calls | Large session payloads being cached in-process as well as in Redis | Known issue: see JIRA IAM-388 (not yet fixed as of this writing)
escalation: If pod is restarting more than once per hour, page the identity on-call. Auth instability cascades to all other services.

Runbook auth AUTH-003 step 1: Confirm OOMKill
command: kubectl describe pod -l app=auth-service | grep -A5 'Last State'
note:

Runbook auth AUTH-003 step 2: Check memory limit and current usage
command: kubectl top pods -l app=auth-service
note: Current memory limit is 512Mi. If consistently above 400Mi, consider bumping to 768Mi as a short-term workaround. File ticket for proper investigation.

Runbook auth AUTH-003 step 3: Take heap dump before restarting (if pod is still running)
command: kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- python -c "import tracemalloc; tracemalloc.start()" 2>/dev/null || echo 'Use py-spy or attach debugger'
note: Honestly this is hard to do in production. If you can, get a heap snapshot. Otherwise just bump the memory limit and file a proper bug.

Runbook auth AUTH-003 step 4: Temporarily increase memory limit to stabilise
command: kubectl set resources deployment/auth-service --limits=memory=768Mi
note: This buys time. Do NOT do this repeatedly without investigating root cause.

Runbook auth AUTH-003 step 5: Check connection pool configuration
command: kubectl exec deploy/auth-service -- env | grep -i pool
note: Look for DB_POOL_SIZE and REDIS_POOL_SIZE. These should not be unbounded. If they are, set them. See config docs — or ask Alice.

Runbook auth — AUTH-004: Account lockout not working / brute force attempts
symptoms: Spike in /auth/login requests from few IPs | Password reset volume unusually high | Lockout counter not incrementing despite repeated failures
likely causes: Lockout state stored per-pod in-memory instead of Redis (known bug, may or may not be fixed in current version) | Rate limiting configured on global IP rather than per-user | WAF not blocking repeated IPs
escalation: Active brute force against production is a security incident. Notify security-team@example.com and page identity on-call.

Runbook auth AUTH-004 step 1: Check if lockout is hitting Redis or in-memory
command: kubectl exec deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST keys 'lockout:*' | head -20
note: If you see lockout keys in Redis, locking is working. If empty during an active brute force, it's in-memory (broken).

Runbook auth AUTH-004 step 2: Block attacking IPs at WAF level immediately
command: Log into Cloudflare dashboard > Security > WAF > Custom Rules > Add IP block rule
note: This is the fastest mitigation. Get the attacking IPs from gateway logs: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep 'POST /auth/login' | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head

Runbook auth AUTH-004 step 3: Temporarily tighten rate limit on login endpoint
command: Update gateway rate limit config for /auth/login — set to 5 req/min per IP. See gateway runbook for how to do this.
note: Be careful not to lock out legitimate users. Coordinate with customer support.

Runbook auth AUTH-004 step 4: Check if AUTH_LOCKOUT_BACKEND env var is set to 'redis'
command: kubectl get deploy/auth-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name=="AUTH_LOCKOUT_BACKEND")'
note: If missing or set to 'memory', this is the bug. Add the env var and redeploy. Check with Alice first.

Runbook auth — AUTH-005: TOTP / MFA codes being rejected
symptoms: Users with MFA enabled cannot log in | Logs show 'TOTP validation failed' for codes that should be valid | Only affects MFA users, regular password login works
likely causes: Auth-service host clock drift — NTP sync issue (most common) | TOTP window tolerance too narrow | VM was suspended and resumed without re-syncing clock
escalation: If NTP sync doesn't resolve it and drift isn't the issue, escalate to #identity-team. Don't guess at TOTP seed issues.

Runbook auth AUTH-005 step 1: Check clock drift on auth-service pods
command: for pod in $(kubectl get pods -l app=auth-service -o name); do echo $pod; kubectl exec $pod -- date -u; done
note: Compare each pod time against actual UTC. TOTP has a 30s window (1 step). Drift over 30s will reject valid codes. Drift over 90s will reject even with generous window.

Runbook auth AUTH-005 step 2: Force NTP sync if drifted
command: kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- chronyc -a makestep
note: This only fixes the running pod. If it's a VM image issue, the drift will return after next pod restart. File infra ticket.

Runbook auth AUTH-005 step 3: Temporarily widen TOTP tolerance window
command: kubectl set env deployment/auth-service TOTP_STEP_WINDOW=2
note: Default is 1 (±30s). Setting to 2 gives ±60s. Setting above 3 is not recommended for security reasons. This is a temporary fix.

Runbook auth AUTH-005 step 4: Verify fix by checking recent TOTP success rate
command: kubectl logs deploy/auth-service | grep 'TOTP' | grep -c 'success\|fail'
note: Ratio should flip back to mostly successes. If not, the issue is something else — possibly a bug in the TOTP seed storage.

Runbook gateway — GW-001: All API endpoints returning 502 / 503
symptoms: External uptime checks firing from multiple regions | 100% of requests failing | Cloudflare dashboard showing origin errors | Customers reporting site is down
likely causes: Upstream service(s) unhealthy — gateway correctly returning 5xx when upstreams fail (check each upstream before assuming gateway issue) | Gateway pods themselves crashed (less common) | Cloudflare misconfiguration or Cloudflare incident | SSL certificate expired | ALB health check failure causing instance deregistration
escalation: If more than 50% of endpoints are failing for more than 2 minutes, this is P1. Page platform on-call. Notify #incidents channel with initial assessment.

Runbook gateway GW-001 step 1: Check gateway pods are running
command: kubectl get pods -l app=gateway
note: If pods are healthy, the problem is almost certainly an upstream. If pods are crashing, check logs first.

Runbook gateway GW-001 step 2: Check which upstreams are healthy
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/upstream-health | jq .
note: This lists all upstream health. UNHEALTHY means the circuit breaker has opened for that upstream. A single unhealthy upstream doesn't cause all 502s — check which endpoints are failing.

Runbook gateway GW-001 step 3: Check SSL certificate validity
command: echo | openssl s_client -connect api.example.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
note: If cert is expired, everything HTTPS will fail. Renew immediately using the cert renewal runbook (find it on Confluence — search 'TLS renewal'). This has a 14-day warning alert that should have fired.

Runbook gateway GW-001 step 4: Check Cloudflare status
command: curl -s https://www.cloudflarestatus.com/api/v2/status.json | jq .status.description
note: If Cloudflare is having an incident, we can't do much. Monitor their status page. In a true Cloudflare outage, direct traffic to origin is possible but not recommended without security review.

Runbook gateway GW-001 step 5: Check ALB target group health
command: aws elbv2 describe-target-health --target-group-arn <gateway-tg-arn> | jq '.TargetHealthDescriptions[] | {id: .Target.Id, state: .TargetHealth.State}'
note: If targets are draining or unhealthy, the gateway is being deregistered. Check the /health endpoint response time — if it's > 2 seconds, ALB marks it unhealthy. See GW-006.

Runbook gateway — GW-002: Circuit breaker open for an upstream service
symptoms: Specific set of endpoints returning 503 with Retry-After header | Datadog shows 'circuit_breaker_state=OPEN' for a service | Logs contain 'circuit breaker OPEN for <service-name>'
likely causes: Upstream service is genuinely degraded — circuit breaker working correctly | Circuit breaker threshold too low (failure_threshold=1 has caused false opens before — see INC-4021) | Transient network blip caused spike in errors that tripped the breaker
escalation: Circuit breaker open for payments-service is P1 — page both platform and payments on-call. Other services are P2.

Runbook gateway GW-002 step 1: Identify which circuit breaker is open
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/status | jq .
note: Note the state (OPEN, HALF_OPEN, CLOSED) and the error_count and failure_threshold for each upstream.

Runbook gateway GW-002 step 2: Check if the upstream is actually unhealthy
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://<upstream-service>.svc/health
note: If the upstream's own health endpoint returns 200, the breaker may have tripped on a transient error. If it returns 5xx or times out, the upstream is genuinely down — fix the upstream, not the breaker.

Runbook gateway GW-002 step 3: Check failure_threshold configuration
command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .circuit_breaker'
note: failure_threshold should be 5. If it's 1, this is the misconfiguration from INC-4021. Change it to 5 and redeploy. This has been a recurring issue.

Runbook gateway GW-002 step 4: If upstream is healthy, manually reset the circuit breaker
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s -X POST localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/<service-name>/reset
note: Only do this if you have confirmed the upstream is healthy. The breaker will re-open within seconds if the upstream is still bad.

Runbook gateway GW-002 step 5: Monitor for 5 minutes after reset
command: watch -n 5 'kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/status | jq'
note: If the breaker re-opens, the upstream is still unhealthy. Stop resetting and focus on the upstream.

Runbook gateway — GW-003: Rate limiting blocking legitimate users
symptoms: Specific users or API keys getting 429 errors | Enterprise customers complaining about throttling | High 429 rate in Datadog without corresponding bot traffic spike
likely causes: Rate limit threshold too low for the traffic pattern | Rate limiter keying on wrong field (IP instead of API key, or vice versa) | Rate limiter reading from wrong IP header — if using remote_addr instead of X-Real-IP, all traffic looks like it comes from the load balancer IP and gets throttled together | Legitimate enterprise users exceeding standard tier limits
escalation: If rate limiting is causing revenue impact (enterprise customers blocked), notify Carol and the account manager for the affected customer.

Runbook gateway GW-003 step 1: Identify who is being rate limited
command: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep '429' | awk '{print $NF}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
note: This shows what's being rate limited. If everything is from one IP (10.0.0.x), the rate limiter is reading from the wrong header — it's seeing the load balancer IP.

Runbook gateway GW-003 step 2: Check which IP header the rate limiter is using
command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .rate_limit.ip_header'
note: If it's 'remote_addr', change it. This caused an incident before where bot traffic bypassed rate limiting entirely because all requests appeared to be from the same internal IP.

Runbook gateway GW-003 step 3: Check rate limit tiers for affected user/API key
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/rate-limit/status/<api-key> | jq .
note: Shows current tier (standard=100/min, enterprise=2000/min) and current usage. If an enterprise customer is on the standard tier, that's a configuration error — update their tier.

Runbook gateway GW-003 step 4: Temporarily whitelist an affected API key if urgent
command: kubectl set env deployment/gateway RATE_LIMIT_WHITELIST=<api-key>
note: This is a hacky fix. Comma-separate multiple keys. Restart required. Use only for genuine emergencies. File a proper fix ticket.

Runbook gateway — GW-004: CORS errors for specific clients or routes
symptoms: Browser console showing CORS errors | Preflight OPTIONS requests returning incorrect headers | Affects specific browsers (often Safari specifically) | Works from Postman but not from browser
likely causes: Missing Access-Control-Allow headers on OPTIONS response | Vary: Origin header not set — CDN caching CORS responses incorrectly | New endpoint added without CORS config | Safari has stricter CORS preflight caching than Chrome — a config that works in Chrome may fail in Safari
escalation: CORS errors are usually P3 unless they're blocking a key user flow. Notify #platform-eng during business hours.

Runbook gateway GW-004 step 1: Reproduce the CORS error with curl
command: curl -v -X OPTIONS https://api.example.com/<failing-path> -H 'Origin: https://app.example.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: POST'
note: Check the response headers. You should see Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, and Vary: Origin.

Runbook gateway GW-004 step 2: Check gateway CORS configuration
command: kubectl get configmap gateway-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .cors'
note: Verify that the failing route is included in the CORS rules. New routes are not automatically CORS-enabled.

Runbook gateway GW-004 step 3: Add CORS config for missing route
command: Edit gateway-config configmap and add the route to the cors.allowed_routes list. Then: kubectl rollout restart deployment/gateway
note: Rollout restart is zero-downtime but will cause brief latency spike. Do during off-peak if possible. Changes to configmap take effect after pod restart.

Runbook gateway GW-004 step 4: If Cloudflare is caching CORS responses, purge cache
command: Log into Cloudflare > Caching > Cache Purge > Purge by URL for the affected endpoint
note: Cloudflare should not be caching responses without Vary: Origin. If it is, check the Cloudflare cache rules for that route.

Runbook gateway — GW-005: Logging / observability gaps — missing client IPs or log entries
symptoms: All access logs showing 10.0.0.1 as client IP | Grafana dashboards showing zero or flat metrics | Log aggregation showing gaps in log timeline
likely causes: Gateway logging using remote_addr instead of X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP | Metrics exporter misconfigured or Prometheus scrape job failing | Fluentd overwhelmed and dropping logs during high-volume periods | Metric name changed in a refactor but Grafana dashboard not updated
escalation: Observability gaps during an incident are significant — escalate to Carol. Log loss during incidents is a post-mortem finding.

Runbook gateway GW-005 step 1: Check what IP is being logged
command: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep 'access' | tail -5 | jq '.client_ip'
note: If you see 10.0.0.x consistently, it's the load balancer IP. The log format needs to use $http_x_real_ip or $http_x_forwarded_for.

Runbook gateway GW-005 step 2: Check Prometheus scrape target for gateway
command: kubectl port-forward deploy/prometheus 9090 & sleep 2 && curl -s localhost:9090/api/v1/targets | jq '.data.activeTargets[] | select(.labels.job=="gateway") | {health, lastScrape}'
note: If health is 'down', Prometheus can't reach the gateway metrics endpoint. Check that /metrics is accessible from within the cluster.

Runbook gateway GW-005 step 3: Check Fluentd buffer and drop rate
command: kubectl logs -l app=fluentd | grep -i 'drop\|buffer\|retry' | tail -20
note: Fluentd drops logs when the output buffer is full. During incidents, this can cause significant log gaps. Fluentd buffer was increased after INC-4021 but may still be insufficient under extreme load.

Runbook gateway GW-005 step 4: Check Grafana dashboard queries against actual metric names
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -s localhost:8080/metrics | grep -i 'request_duration'
note: Compare metric names from /metrics endpoint against what's in the Grafana query. After a refactor in late May, some metrics were renamed and dashboards were not updated. This has happened more than once.

Runbook gateway — GW-006: ALB marking gateway instances as unhealthy
symptoms: Instances being deregistered from ALB | Request errors increasing as capacity drops | ALB access logs showing health check failures | gateway pod is running fine but ALB shows it as unhealthy
likely causes: /health endpoint is too slow (doing DB check or heavy computation) and exceeds ALB's 2-second timeout | Pod is genuinely overwhelmed and can't respond to health checks fast enough | ALB health check misconfigured (wrong port, wrong path)
escalation: If instances are being rapidly deregistered and traffic is failing, this is P1. Page platform on-call.

Runbook gateway GW-006 step 1: Check /health endpoint response time
command: kubectl exec deploy/gateway -- curl -w '%{time_total}' -o /dev/null -s localhost:8080/health
note: If the health endpoint is doing a database query or checking upstream connectivity, it's doing too much. Health check should be shallow — just return 200 if the process is alive.

Runbook gateway GW-006 step 2: Check ALB health check configuration
command: aws elbv2 describe-target-groups --target-group-arns <arn> | jq '.TargetGroups[0] | {HealthCheckPath, HealthCheckPort, HealthCheckIntervalSeconds, HealthCheckTimeoutSeconds, UnhealthyThresholdCount}'
note: HealthCheckTimeoutSeconds should be >= 5. If it's 2 and our health endpoint takes 3 seconds sometimes, we'll have false failures.

Runbook gateway GW-006 step 3: Temporarily increase ALB timeout if needed
command: aws elbv2 modify-target-group --target-group-arn <arn> --health-check-timeout-seconds 10
note: This buys time. The real fix is making the /health endpoint return immediately. There's a config option for shallow health check — check gateway config docs or ask Carol.

Runbook gateway GW-006 step 4: Switch /health to shallow check
command: kubectl set env deployment/gateway HEALTH_CHECK_MODE=shallow
note: If this env var exists, it disables the upstream connectivity checks in /health. Use /health/deep for internal monitoring only. This should have been done already — check why it wasn't.

Runbook orders-db — ORDDB-001: Disk quota exceeded — writes failing
symptoms: orders-service returning 500 on all write operations | Postgres logs: 'could not write to file... No space left on device' | Alert fired: 'orders-db disk usage > 90%' | pg_wal directory unexpectedly large
likely causes: WAL archive cron not running — WAL files accumulate in pg_wal indefinitely | S3 bucket for WAL archive unreachable — archive command failing silently | Unusual write volume (e.g., during incident with high retry rates) | Table bloat from dead tuples not being VACUUMed
escalation: Disk full on orders-db is P1 — order writes are failing. Page commerce on-call immediately. This is time-sensitive.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 1: Confirm disk is full and identify largest directories
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- df -h && du -sh /var/lib/postgresql/data/*/
note: Check which directory is largest. Usually it's pg_wal or the main data directory. pg_wal being unexpectedly large = WAL archive problem.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 2: Check WAL archiving status
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT archived_count, failed_count, last_archived_wal, last_failed_wal, last_failed_time FROM pg_stat_archiver;"
note: failed_count > 0 and last_failed_time recent = archive is failing. last_archived_wal being very old = archive hasn't run in a while. Either way, WAL is accumulating on disk.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 3: Check if archive cron is running
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- crontab -l && grep -i wal /var/log/syslog | tail -20
note: The WAL archive cron should run every hour. If there's no crontab entry, it may have been wiped during a pod restart. This was the root cause of INC-4021.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 4: Prune WAL archive to free space — DO THIS CAREFULLY
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- pg_archivecleanup /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/ $(psql -U postgres -t -c "SELECT pg_walfile_name(pg_current_wal_lsn());")
note: pg_archivecleanup is safe to run. It only removes WAL files that are no longer needed for recovery. Do NOT manually rm files from pg_wal — you can corrupt the cluster. Get a second pair of eyes before running this.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 5: Extend EBS volume if pruning isn't enough
command: aws ec2 modify-volume --volume-id <volume-id> --size <new-size-gb>
note: You can extend EBS volumes online without downtime. After extending, you need to resize the filesystem too: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- resize2fs /dev/xvda. Volume ID is in the PVC — kubectl get pvc orders-db-pvc -o json | jq '.spec.volumeName'

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-001 step 6: Restart Postgres after recovery to clear write errors
command: kubectl rollout restart statefulset/orders-db
note: Postgres may be in a bad state after a disk-full event. Restart forces clean connection pool and clears cached errors. Monitor logs after restart.

Runbook orders-db — ORDDB-002: Slow queries / high DB CPU
symptoms: orders-service request latency elevated | Postgres CPU > 70% sustained | pg_stat_statements showing queries > 1 second | Admin dashboard timing out
likely causes: Missing index on heavily-queried columns | Sequential scan on orders table (40M+ rows — very expensive without index) | analytics dashboard firing 14 concurrent heavy queries (known issue) | VACUUM not running — table bloat causing planner to make bad choices | N+1 query pattern from orders-service (fetches order then fetches each item separately)
escalation: If queries are causing customer-facing timeouts, escalate to P1. Otherwise P2 during business hours.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-002 step 1: Identify slow queries
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT substring(query, 1, 100) as query, calls, round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) as mean_ms, round(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) as total_ms FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;"
note: pg_stat_statements must be in shared_preload_libraries. If you get an error, it's not enabled. The fix requires a restart — coordinate with Dave.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-002 step 2: EXPLAIN the slow query
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) <paste_slow_query_here>;"
note: Look for 'Seq Scan' on the orders table. If you see it, there's a missing index. 'Bitmap Heap Scan' is OK. 'Index Scan' is ideal.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-002 step 4: If analytics dashboard is culprit, disable it
command: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service ANALYTICS_DASHBOARD_ENABLED=false
note: There's a ticket to rewrite the analytics queries against a materialized view. JIRA COM-512. Not done yet. Disabling the dashboard is the only mitigation for now.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-002 step 5: Run VACUUM if table bloat is suspected
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "VACUUM ANALYZE orders;"
note: VACUUM ANALYZE is safe to run concurrently with normal operations (not VACUUM FULL). It reclaims dead tuple space and updates statistics. Run during off-peak. Takes 10-30 minutes on a large table.

Runbook orders-db — ORDDB-003: Replication lag / replica falling behind
symptoms: Replica shows stale data compared to primary | Monitoring alert: 'replication_lag > 30s' | Read queries returning outdated results
likely causes: High write volume on primary — replica can't apply WAL fast enough | Network congestion between primary and replica AZ | Replica under-resourced (less CPU/memory than primary) | Long-running query on replica holding back apply process
escalation: Replication lag > 5 minutes is P2. Lag > 30 minutes or replication stopped entirely is P1 — page DBA on-call.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-003 step 1: Check replication lag
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT client_addr, state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn, (sent_lsn - replay_lsn) AS replication_delay FROM pg_stat_replication;"
note: replication_delay in bytes. Divide by ~1MB to get rough seconds of lag. If delay is 0, replica is caught up.

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-003 step 2: Check if read traffic is being routed to lagging replica
command: kubectl get configmap orders-db-config -o json | jq '.data | fromjson | .read_replica_enabled'
note: If read_replica_enabled is true and replica is lagging, reads may see stale data. Temporarily disable read routing: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service DB_READ_REPLICA_ENABLED=false

Runbook orders-db ORDDB-003 step 3: Check for blocking queries on replica
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-replica-0 -- psql -U postgres -c "SELECT pid, query_start, state, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle' ORDER BY query_start ASC LIMIT 10;"
note: Long-running queries on the replica can block WAL apply. Terminate if safe to do so.

Runbook orders — ORD-001: Orders failing to create / 500 on POST /orders
symptoms: HTTP 500 on POST /api/v1/orders | Error rate spike on 'Orders Creation' Datadog dashboard | Support tickets: customers completing checkout but no order appearing | orders-service logs showing exception stack traces
likely causes: payments-service unavailable — orders cannot be placed without payment confirmation | auth-service returning errors — token validation failing | orders-db at capacity (disk full, connection pool exhausted) | inventory-service timeout — stock reservation failing | Kafka producer failing — usually non-blocking but can cause issues in some code paths
escalation: Order creation failures are P1 during business hours. Page commerce on-call. If payments-service is the root cause, also page payments on-call.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 1: Check orders-service pod logs for the actual error
command: kubectl logs -l app=orders-service --since=10m | grep -i 'error\|exception\|failed' | tail -30
note: The error message usually tells you exactly which dependency failed. Don't guess — read the logs first.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 2: Check all hard dependencies are healthy
command: for svc in auth-service payments-service inventory-service; do echo "=== $svc ==="; kubectl get pods -l app=$svc; done
note: Any CrashLoopBackOff or Pending pods in dependencies will cascade to orders. Fix the dependency first.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 3: Check orders-db disk usage
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size('orders'));"
note: If this times out, the DB might already be in trouble. Check the disk alert in Datadog. Disk full = all writes fail silently. This is what happened in INC-4021.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 4: Check WAL archive size if disk is high
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- du -sh /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/
note: WAL directory growing unexpectedly large suggests the archive cron job is not running. Check cron logs. Last time this happened we had 47 days of unarchived WAL. See INC-4021.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 5: If WAL is the problem, prune carefully
command: kubectl exec -it orders-db-primary-0 -- pg_archivecleanup /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_wal/ <last_known_good_wal_file>
note: Do NOT just delete files from pg_wal manually. Use pg_archivecleanup. Get a second set of eyes on this before proceeding. If unsure, escalate to DBA on-call.

Runbook orders ORD-001 step 6: Check Kafka producer health if dependency services are fine
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/kafka/status | jq .
note: Kafka failures should be logged but non-fatal for order creation. If the service is treating Kafka failure as fatal, that's a bug.

Runbook orders — ORD-002: Orders stuck in 'Processing' status
symptoms: Orders created but never transition to CONFIRMED or SHIPPED | Kafka consumer lag growing on orders.created topic | Fulfillment emails not being sent | orders-service logs show orders created successfully but downstream silent
likely causes: Kafka consumer lag — downstream consumer (notifications, fulfillment) is backed up | Poison-pill message blocking a Kafka partition (deserialization failure on one message stops the whole partition) | notifications-service or fulfillment-service is down | Orders not actually missing — just delayed due to consumer lag
escalation: If consumer lag exceeds 100,000 messages or orders have been stuck for over an hour, escalate to #commerce-platform and page commerce on-call.

Runbook orders ORD-002 step 1: Check Kafka consumer lag for orders.created topic
command: kubectl exec -it kafka-0 -- kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --describe --group notifications-service-cg
note: Look at the LAG column. A few hundred is normal. Tens of thousands means something is blocking consumption. Note: there's also a fulfillment consumer group — check that too. Can't remember the exact group name, check with Dave.

Runbook orders ORD-002 step 2: Check for poison-pill messages
command: kubectl logs -l app=notifications-service | grep -i 'deserializ\|parse\|error' | tail -20
note: A deserialization error repeating on the same offset means the consumer is stuck on a bad message. The message offset will be in the log.

Runbook orders ORD-002 step 3: Skip the poison-pill message if confirmed
command: kubectl exec -it kafka-0 -- kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --group notifications-service-cg --topic orders.created --reset-offsets --to-offset <bad_offset+1> --execute
note: Only do this if you have confirmed the message is malformed and cannot be processed. The skipped message goes to the dead-letter queue if DLQ is configured. Check that it's configured first — it was added after INC-4021 but may not be on all consumer groups.

Runbook orders ORD-002 step 4: Check if orders are actually lost or just delayed
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT id, status, created_at FROM orders WHERE status = 'PROCESSING' AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 minutes' LIMIT 20;"
note: Orders more than 30 minutes in PROCESSING state are genuinely stuck. Orders less than 30 minutes old might just be delayed by consumer lag. Don't panic if it's the latter.

Runbook orders ORD-002 step 5: Manually reprocess stuck orders if needed
command: There's a script for this somewhere — ask Dave. It's not in this runbook yet. TODO: add it.
note: Last time we needed to reprocess, Dave ran it manually. It re-publishes the orders.created events for specific order IDs. Very much a last resort.

Runbook orders — ORD-003: Inventory going negative / overselling
symptoms: inventory-service shows negative stock count for a SKU | Multiple customers ordered same last item simultaneously | Orders confirmed for items that should have been out of stock
likely causes: Race condition: check-and-decrement on inventory is not atomic | Inventory reservation call is async or being skipped under load | Cache serving stale stock count — Redis cache not invalidated on purchase
escalation: Overselling is a business-impacting incident. Involve commerce on-call and product lead immediately. Do not make unilateral decisions about order cancellations.

Runbook orders ORD-003 step 1: Identify affected SKUs
command: kubectl exec deploy/inventory-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT sku_id, stock_count FROM inventory WHERE stock_count < 0;"
note: Get the list of negative-stock SKUs. Cross-reference with orders to understand how many customers are affected.

Runbook orders ORD-003 step 2: Temporarily set affected SKUs to unavailable
command: kubectl exec deploy/inventory-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "UPDATE inventory SET available = false WHERE sku_id IN ('<sku_ids>');"
note: This prevents more overselling while you investigate. Coordinate with product team before doing this — they may prefer a different approach.

Runbook orders ORD-003 step 3: Check if inventory reservation is using atomic operations
command: Look at inventory-service source code, reservation handler. It should use a SELECT FOR UPDATE or equivalent. If not, that's the bug.
note: This is a code review task, not a kubectl task. The fix requires a deploy. In the meantime, scaling inventory-service down to 1 pod reduces (but doesn't eliminate) the race.

Runbook orders ORD-003 step 4: Decide what to do with oversold orders
command: N/A — this is a business decision, not a technical one.
note: Options: (1) honour all orders and fulfil from back-stock/reorder. (2) Cancel excess orders with apology and voucher. (3) Something else. Get Dave and product lead on a call.

Runbook orders — ORD-004: Duplicate orders created on payment retry
symptoms: Customer reports being shipped two identical orders | orders-db shows two orders with same cart session but different order IDs | Stripe shows one successful charge but two fulfillment events
likely causes: Idempotency token missing on order creation endpoint | Frontend retried after timeout and created second order with new idempotency token | Payment retry logic creating new order instead of resuming existing one
escalation: If more than 5 customers affected, notify customer support lead to prepare outreach. Notify Dave.

Runbook orders ORD-004 step 1: Confirm duplication in orders-db
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT cart_session_id, count(*), array_agg(id) FROM orders GROUP BY cart_session_id HAVING count(*) > 1 ORDER BY count(*) DESC LIMIT 10;"
note: If you see multiple orders per cart_session_id, that's your duplicate. Check the timestamps — usually within seconds of each other.

Runbook orders ORD-004 step 2: Cancel the duplicate orders
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "UPDATE orders SET status = 'CANCELLED', cancellation_reason = 'duplicate' WHERE id = '<duplicate_order_id>';"
note: Cancel the later one unless there's a reason to prefer it. Make sure any shipped items are intercepted if possible — coordinate with fulfillment.

Runbook orders ORD-004 step 3: Check if idempotency is enforced on order creation
command: kubectl logs deploy/orders-service | grep 'idempotency\|cart_session' | tail -20
note: Should see idempotency key logged for each order creation. If absent, the feature may not be deployed or is misconfigured.

Runbook orders ORD-004 step 4: Check current idempotency config
command: kubectl get deploy/orders-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name | contains("IDEMPOTENCY"))'
note: ORDER_IDEMPOTENCY_ENABLED should be true. If missing, feature is off. Was added as a fix post-INC-3844 — should be present in v4.14.0+.

Runbook orders — ORD-005: Order data visible across tenants / privacy leak
symptoms: Customer reports seeing another customer's orders | Admin export contains orders from all tenants | GET /orders returns more results than expected for a user
likely causes: Missing tenant_id filter in query (most likely after a migration or new endpoint was added) | Caching layer not scoped to user/tenant context | Admin export endpoint missing tenancy clause
escalation: This is a P0 security incident. Page security on-call, notify Dave, and alert legal team. Do not discuss in public Slack channels.

Runbook orders ORD-005 step 1: STOP — do not investigate casually. This is a data privacy incident.
command: N/A
note: Immediately notify security-team@example.com and alice@example.com. Document what you know. Do not access data you're not authorised to access during investigation.

Runbook orders ORD-005 step 2: Disable the affected endpoint immediately
command: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service ORDER_EXPORT_ENABLED=false
note: Or set a feature flag to disable. The priority is stopping data exposure, not understanding it.

Runbook orders ORD-005 step 3: Identify how long the leak has been active
command: kubectl logs deploy/orders-service --since=24h | grep '<affected_endpoint>' | wc -l
note: You need to know the window of exposure for GDPR notification purposes. Preserve logs — do not allow them to rotate.

Runbook orders ORD-005 step 4: Identify which customers' data was exposed
command: Access logs from the affected endpoint. Correlate requesting user with order data returned. This will require a forensic query — get DBA on-call involved.
note: This is for GDPR breach notification. Legal needs to know within 72 hours if this is a reportable breach. Do not delay.

Runbook orders — ORD-006: Slow order queries / dashboard timeouts
symptoms: Admin dashboard timing out when loading order lists | GET /orders endpoint P99 > 5 seconds | orders-db CPU spiking to 90%+ during dashboard load | Postgres slow query log filling up
likely causes: Missing index on frequently queried columns (status, created_at, customer_id) | Analytics dashboard firing too many queries simultaneously | A new query added without EXPLAIN ANALYZE check | Table bloat from high write volume not being VACUUMed
escalation: Slow queries causing customer-facing timeouts are P2. If affecting all users, escalate to P1 and page commerce on-call.

Runbook orders ORD-006 step 1: Identify slow queries
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10;"
note: pg_stat_statements must be enabled. If you get 'relation not found', it's not enabled — ask DBA. Alternatively check the slow query log: kubectl exec orders-db-primary-0 -- tail -100 /var/log/postgresql/postgresql.log

Runbook orders ORD-006 step 2: Check indexes on orders table
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname, indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'orders';"
note: Should have indexes on: customer_id, created_at, status, and ideally composite (customer_id, created_at DESC). If the composite index is missing, add it — but use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid locking.

Runbook orders ORD-006 step 3: Add missing index without locking
command: kubectl exec deploy/orders-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_customer_created ON orders(customer_id, created_at DESC);"
note: CONCURRENTLY means it doesn't lock the table. Takes longer but safe for production. Monitor orders-db CPU while it runs.

Runbook orders ORD-006 step 4: If analytics dashboard is the culprit, disable or rate-limit it
command: kubectl set env deployment/orders-service ANALYTICS_DASHBOARD_ENABLED=false
note: The analytics dashboard queries are known to be expensive. There's a ticket to rewrite them against a materialized view but it hasn't been done yet. See JIRA COM-512.

Runbook payments — PAY-001: Payments timing out / checkout hanging
symptoms: POST /api/v1/payments returns 504 or hangs indefinitely | Stripe dashboard shows no incoming requests (suggests we're not reaching Stripe) | Circuit breaker metric in Datadog showing OPEN state | Customer reports card charged but no order confirmation
likely causes: Stripe API is degraded — check status.stripe.com first before doing anything else | Circuit breaker opened due to repeated failures and is now rejecting all requests | payments-service pods in CrashLoopBackOff due to DB or secret issue | Payments-db unreachable or pool exhausted
escalation: Any payment processing outage lasting more than 5 minutes is a P1. Page payments on-call and notify #payments-eng. If customers were charged but orders not created, that is also a P1 — involve customer support lead.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 1: Check Stripe status page immediately
command: curl -s https://www.stripestatus.com/api/v2/status.json | jq .status.description
note: If Stripe is degraded, this is likely their fault. Monitor their incident. Our circuit breaker will auto-reset when Stripe recovers. Do not restart pods for a Stripe outage.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 2: Check circuit breaker state
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- curl -s localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/status | jq .
note: State should be CLOSED (healthy). OPEN means it's blocking all requests. HALF_OPEN means it's testing recovery. If OPEN, check why it opened — look at recent error logs.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 3: Check pod status
command: kubectl get pods -l app=payments-service
note: Any CrashLoopBackOff pods are a problem. If you see this, do NOT restart — first check what the crash reason is.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 4: Check pod crash logs
command: kubectl logs -l app=payments-service --previous | tail -50
note: Look for DB connection errors, secret errors, or SSL errors. 'cannot connect to postgres' after a deploy usually means DB credentials are wrong in the k8s secret.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 5: Verify DB credentials secret is correct
command: kubectl get secret payments-db-credentials -o json | jq '.data | map_values(@base64d)'
note: Check that the host points to PRODUCTION postgres, not staging. This has happened before (INC-4021). sslmode must be 'require'.

Runbook payments PAY-001 step 6: If Stripe is fine and pods are healthy, manually reset circuit breaker
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- curl -s -X POST localhost:8080/internal/circuit-breaker/reset
note: Only do this if you have confirmed the upstream (Stripe) is healthy. Resetting while Stripe is still degraded will just cause it to open again immediately.

Runbook payments — PAY-002: Duplicate charges or double processing
symptoms: Customer reports being charged twice | payments-db shows two transaction records for same order | Stripe dashboard shows duplicate payment intents for same customer
likely causes: Idempotency key not being set on Stripe API calls (or being generated incorrectly) | Webhook retry from Stripe being processed multiple times | Frontend retrying payment after timeout without idempotency token
escalation: Any confirmed double-charge is a financial incident. Notify bob@example.com and the CFO's team. Do not communicate specifics to customers until legal/finance has reviewed.

Runbook payments PAY-002 step 1: Confirm duplication in payments-db
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT order_id, count(*) FROM transactions GROUP BY order_id HAVING count(*) > 1 LIMIT 10;"
note: Identify affected order_ids. Get exact timestamps of both charges to understand the sequence.

Runbook payments PAY-002 step 2: Check Stripe dashboard for duplicate payment intents
command: Log into Stripe dashboard, search by customer email or order ID. Look for multiple PaymentIntents with identical metadata.
note: If both charges succeeded, you need to refund one immediately. Do not wait.

Runbook payments PAY-002 step 3: Issue manual refund for duplicate charge via Stripe dashboard
command: Stripe dashboard > Payments > find duplicate charge > Refund
note: Document the refund amount and reason in the incident ticket. Notify customer support to reach out to affected customer.

Runbook payments PAY-002 step 4: Check if idempotency key logic is functioning
command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep 'idempotency_key' | tail -20
note: Idempotency key should be set on every Stripe call. If absent from logs, this is a code bug. Pull the relevant PR and check.

Runbook payments PAY-002 step 5: Check Stripe webhook retry config
command: Log into Stripe dashboard > Developers > Webhooks > check retry attempts for recent events
note: If Stripe retried a webhook 3 times and we processed each one, our idempotency check on webhooks isn't working. Check payments-service code for webhook deduplication logic.

Runbook payments — PAY-003: Specific card type failing (e.g., Amex)
symptoms: Subset of users with specific card type getting payment errors | Stripe logs show requests going to wrong endpoint | Error code: 1005 or 'card_not_supported'
likely causes: Card-type routing hardcoded to wrong Stripe endpoint (this happened in v3.17.0 — Amex requires /v1/payment_intents, Visa/MC uses /v1/charges) | Stripe account not enabled for specific card type | Currency / country restriction on card type
escalation: Card-type failures are P2 unless the failure is total (all cards failing). Notify #payments-eng. If financial data integrity is at risk, escalate to P1.

Runbook payments PAY-003 step 1: Identify which card types are failing
command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep -i 'error\|fail' | grep -i 'card\|amex\|visa' | tail -30
note: Stripe error messages usually contain card brand info. Identify the pattern.

Runbook payments PAY-003 step 2: Check which Stripe endpoint is being called for failing card type
command: kubectl logs deploy/payments-service | grep 'stripe request' | grep -i '<card_type>'
note: Amex MUST go to /v1/payment_intents. Visa/MC can use /v1/charges. If Amex is going to /v1/charges, that's your bug. This is a code issue not a config issue.

Runbook payments PAY-003 step 3: Check current deployed version
command: kubectl get deploy/payments-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image'
note: If on v3.17.0, this is a known bug. Roll back to v3.15.2 or forward to v3.18.0+.

Runbook payments PAY-003 step 4: Rollback if on known bad version
command: kubectl rollout undo deployment/payments-service
note: Verify with kubectl rollout status deployment/payments-service. Check payments work after rollback before closing incident.

Runbook payments PAY-003 step 5: Identify affected customers and refund/reprocess
command: Query payments-db for failed transactions in the affected window. Cross-reference with Stripe for error codes.
note: Customers whose Amex was declined may have tried other cards. Check for duplicate successful charges too.

Runbook payments — PAY-004: Refunds being processed incorrectly (wrong amount or duplicated)
symptoms: Customer received refund of incorrect amount | Refund amount 100x expected (unit conversion bug) | Multiple refunds for same transaction
likely causes: Dollar-to-cents conversion applied twice (amounts stored in cents internally, refund API may accept dollars — check API contract) | Stripe webhook retries causing refund handler to run multiple times | Missing idempotency check on refund endpoint
escalation: Any financial integrity issue is escalated to bob@example.com and finance team immediately. Do not attempt to fix refund errors without a second engineer reviewing.

Runbook payments PAY-004 step 1: Stop the bleeding — disable refund endpoint if actively misfiring
command: kubectl set env deployment/payments-service REFUND_ENDPOINT_ENABLED=false
note: Only do this if refunds are actively processing incorrectly and you can't fix immediately. Communicate to support team that refunds are temporarily paused.

Runbook payments PAY-004 step 2: Check refund amounts in payments-db vs Stripe
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT id, order_id, amount_cents, refund_amount_cents, created_at FROM refunds ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;"
note: Compare refund_amount_cents against what's in Stripe dashboard. A 100x discrepancy suggests unit conversion bug.

Runbook payments PAY-004 step 3: Check currency conversion code path
command: Look at the refund handler in payments-service source. Check if amount is converted to cents before being passed to Stripe — Stripe expects amounts in the smallest currency unit (cents for USD).
note: This isn't something you can check at runtime. Pull the code. If you see the value being divided by 100 AND multiplied by 100, that's the bug.

Runbook payments PAY-004 step 4: Recover overpaid refunds via Stripe
command: For overpaid refunds, contact Stripe support. They can sometimes claw back excess amounts if reported quickly.
note: Realistically, if the money has been returned to the customer's card, recovery is very difficult. Finance and legal need to decide how to handle this.

Runbook payments — PAY-005: payments-db write failures / connection pool exhausted
symptoms: Logs show 'connection pool exhausted' or 'too many connections' | Transactions failing at persist step despite Stripe succeeding | pg_stat_activity showing max connections reached
likely causes: Connection pool size too large relative to Postgres max_connections | Connections not being returned to pool after use (leak) | Too many payments-service pods each holding large connection pools | Long-running transactions holding connections
escalation: DB connection exhaustion on payments is P1 — write failures mean lost transactions. Page payments on-call and optionally DBA team.

Runbook payments PAY-005 step 1: Check active connections to payments-db
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname = 'payments';"
note: Postgres max_connections is currently 200. We should not be above 150 under normal load. If above 180, something is leaking.

Runbook payments PAY-005 step 2: Identify long-running or idle connections
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pid, state, query_start, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname='payments' ORDER BY query_start ASC LIMIT 20;"
note: Idle connections older than 10 minutes are suspicious. idle in transaction is particularly bad — means a transaction was opened and not committed or rolled back.

Runbook payments PAY-005 step 3: If connections are stuck, terminate the worst offenders
command: kubectl exec deploy/payments-service -- psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle in transaction' AND query_start < NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes';"
note: Do this carefully. Terminating active transactions will cause in-flight payments to fail and possibly need reprocessing. Get a second opinion first.

Runbook payments PAY-005 step 4: Check DB_POOL_SIZE and DB_POOL_MAX_OVERFLOW settings
command: kubectl get deploy/payments-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name | startswith("DB_"))'
note: Pool size × pod count should be less than 70% of Postgres max_connections. If you have 5 pods each with pool size 50, that's 250 connections — above the limit.

Runbook payments PAY-005 step 5: Short-term fix: scale down payments-service pods to reduce connection count
command: kubectl scale deployment/payments-service --replicas=2
note: This reduces capacity but reduces connections proportionally. Only do this during off-peak hours. Remember to scale back up.

Runbook payments — PAY-006: Nightly bank settlement failing (bank-api errors)
symptoms: Morning alert: 'settlement job failed' in PagerDuty | bank-api returning 4xx or 5xx in nightly job logs | Merchants not receiving expected payouts
likely causes: bank-api credentials expired or rotated without updating k8s secret | Settlement file format rejected (ClearBank occasionally updates their schema without warning) | Network connectivity issue between eu-west-1 and ClearBank endpoints | Payments being processed outside allowed settlement window
escalation: Failed settlement affects merchant payouts. If settlement has not completed by 09:00 UK time, escalate to Bob and notify merchant success team.

Runbook payments PAY-006 step 1: Check the settlement job logs
command: kubectl logs job/nightly-settlement --since=12h | tail -100
note: Look for the specific error from bank-api. 401 = credentials issue. 400 = format issue. 503 = their problem.

Runbook payments PAY-006 step 2: Verify bank-api credentials
command: kubectl get secret bank-api-credentials -o json | jq '.data.api_key | @base64d' | head -c 20
note: Only print the first 20 chars for verification. Compare with what's in the password vault. ClearBank credentials rotate every 90 days — check if rotation is overdue.

Runbook payments PAY-006 step 3: Check ClearBank status / contact their support
command: Check their status page or email your ClearBank TAM. Their support email is in the vendor contacts doc (link in Confluence — ask Bob if you can't find it).
note: ClearBank doesn't have a public status page. Their SLA response time is 4 hours during business hours (UK time).

Runbook payments PAY-006 step 4: Manually retry settlement job if safe to do so
command: kubectl create job --from=cronjob/nightly-settlement manual-settlement-$(date +%Y%m%d)
note: Only retry if you've confirmed the job is idempotent for today's run. Check if any partial settlement was sent — double-settling could cause overpayment to merchants.

Runbook redis — REDIS-001: Redis OOM — out of memory / writes failing
symptoms: auth-service logs: 'OOM command not allowed when used memory > maxmemory' | New session creation failing while reads still work | Redis INFO memory shows used_memory > maxmemory
likely causes: Eviction policy set to noeviction — writes blocked when memory full | Memory limit too low for current session volume | Key expiry not being set on some session types — keys accumulating indefinitely | Memory fragmentation causing effective capacity to be lower than configured
escalation: Redis OOM causing auth write failures is P1 — sessions cannot be created. Page platform on-call. Notify identity team.

Runbook redis REDIS-001 step 1: Check Redis memory state
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli info memory | grep -E 'used_memory_human|maxmemory_human|mem_fragmentation_ratio|maxmemory_policy'
note: Key values: used_memory near maxmemory = near limit. mem_fragmentation_ratio > 1.5 = significant fragmentation. maxmemory_policy = noeviction is dangerous.

Runbook redis REDIS-001 step 2: Immediately change eviction policy to allow writes
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
note: This is a live config change — takes effect immediately. allkeys-lru will evict the least recently used keys when memory is full. This may evict some sessions (users get logged out) but is better than complete write failure. This change does not persist across restarts — update the configmap too.

Runbook redis REDIS-001 step 3: Make the eviction policy change persistent
command: kubectl edit configmap redis-config
note: Find the maxmemory-policy line and change to allkeys-lru. If you can't find the configmap, ask Carol. The in-memory change from step 2 will be lost on pod restart.

Runbook redis REDIS-001 step 4: Increase maxmemory if eviction is too aggressive
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory 8gb
note: Current limit is 4GB. Increasing to 8GB is the quick fix. The node must have available memory — check with 'kubectl top node'. Make this persistent in the configmap. File a capacity ticket.

Runbook redis — REDIS-002: Counter reset after Redis failover / restart
symptoms: Duplicate invoice numbers or IDs after a Redis restart | Rate limit counters reset — users who were throttled can suddenly make requests again | Account lockout counters cleared — brute force protection temporarily bypassed
likely causes: Counters stored in Redis with no persistence or backup — Redis treats them as ephemeral | Redis AOF or RDB persistence not enabled or not recovering correctly | Failover promoted a replica that was slightly behind
escalation: If security-sensitive counters (lockout, rate limit) were reset during an active attack, treat as security incident. Page security team.

Runbook redis REDIS-002 step 1: Confirm counter reset occurred
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli DBSIZE
note: A suspiciously low DBSIZE after a restart/failover confirms the data was lost. Compare against what it was before — check Datadog Redis metrics for 'connected_clients' and 'keyspace_hits'.

Runbook redis REDIS-002 step 2: Check Redis persistence configuration
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli CONFIG GET save && redis-cli CONFIG GET appendonly
note: appendonly should be 'yes'. save should have values like '3600 1 300 100 60 10000'. If appendonly is 'no' and save is empty, Redis is fully in-memory — no persistence at all. That's a configuration error.

Runbook redis REDIS-002 step 3: For invoice number specifically — migrate to UUID or DB-sequence
command: N/A — this is a code change in payments-service.
note: Redis counters should not be used for anything that requires uniqueness guarantees across restarts. Invoice numbers in Redis is an architectural error. This was fixed in payments-service v3.18.x — verify that version is deployed.

Runbook redis REDIS-002 step 4: For rate limit / lockout counters — accept the reset and monitor
command: Monitor auth logs for brute force attempts immediately after a Redis restart.
note: Not much you can do about lost rate limit state in real-time. The main risk is that a brute force attacker who was locked out gets a new window of attempts. Monitor and re-block if needed.

Runbook redis — REDIS-003: Redis cluster node down / split-brain
symptoms: CLUSTERDOWN errors in redis-cli | Some keys inaccessible (wrong slot / redirected to dead node) | Replication lag visible in redis-cli cluster info
likely causes: Node pod crashed or was evicted by Kubernetes | Network partition between cluster nodes | Node ran out of memory and was OOMKilled by kernel
escalation: Redis cluster split-brain is P1. Page platform on-call immediately. Do not attempt to force-write to a degraded cluster.

Runbook redis REDIS-003 step 1: Check cluster state
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep cluster_state
note: cluster_state:fail means the cluster has lost quorum. You need to investigate which nodes are down.

Runbook redis REDIS-003 step 2: List cluster nodes and their states
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster nodes
note: Look for nodes with 'fail' or 'disconnected' flag. Note their IDs.

Runbook redis REDIS-003 step 3: Check if failed node pods are running in Kubernetes
command: kubectl get pods -l app=redis
note: If a Redis pod is in CrashLoopBackOff or Pending, that's your failed node. Fix the pod first, then let the cluster auto-recover.

Runbook redis REDIS-003 step 4: If node is running but cluster thinks it's down, try manual failover
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster reset soft
note: Try soft reset first. If that doesn't work, you're in deeper trouble than this runbook covers. Get Carol on the call.

Runbook redis REDIS-003 step 5: After recovery, verify all shards have quorum
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli cluster info | grep -E 'cluster_size|cluster_known_nodes|cluster_slots_ok'
note: cluster_slots_ok should equal 16384 (total slots). Any missing slots = data loss risk.

Runbook redis — REDIS-004: High Redis latency / slow commands
symptoms: auth-service p99 latency elevated even when auth pods are healthy | Redis LATENCY HISTORY showing spikes | SLOWLOG entries accumulating
likely causes: Large key scan operation blocking the single-threaded event loop | High memory fragmentation causing GC-like pauses | KEYS * command being run by someone — never do this in production | Network latency between auth-service and Redis (cross-AZ traffic)
escalation: Redis latency affecting auth is P2. Notify #platform-eng. If p99 auth latency exceeds 1 second, escalate to P1.

Runbook redis REDIS-004 step 1: Check Redis slow log
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli SLOWLOG GET 10
note: Shows recent slow commands with execution time in microseconds. KEYS *, SMEMBERS on large sets, or SORT commands are usual suspects.

Runbook redis REDIS-004 step 2: Check if KEYS * is being used anywhere
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli MONITOR | head -100
note: MONITOR is extremely high overhead — only run for a few seconds. KEYS * in production is forbidden — it blocks all other operations. If you see it, find who's running it and stop them immediately.

Runbook redis REDIS-004 step 3: Check memory fragmentation
command: kubectl exec -it redis-0 -- redis-cli info memory | grep mem_fragmentation_ratio
note: Ratio > 1.5 is concerning, > 2.0 is bad. You can trigger a defrag: redis-cli CONFIG SET activedefrag yes. This runs in the background and reduces fragmentation over time.

Runbook redis REDIS-004 step 4: Check cross-AZ traffic if latency is consistent
command: Check whether auth-service pods are in the same AZ as the Redis primary. kubectl get pods -l app=auth-service -o wide (check node). kubectl get pods -l app=redis-0 -o wide (check node).
note: Cross-AZ Redis calls add 1-3ms per call. With many calls per request, this adds up. Ideally, auth-service and Redis primary should be in the same AZ. Topology-aware routing is a future improvement — JIRA PLAT-189.

Incident Event Log — Schema Reference
======================================

This file documents the columns in incident_event_log.csv.
All timestamps use ISO 8601 UTC format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ).
Service names match the canonical IDs in service_dependency_graph.json.
Incident IDs match the incident_id field in incident_tickets.json and production_logs.json.
Columns:

1. incident_id
   Unique identifier for the incident (e.g., INC-4019, INC-4021, INC-3908).
   Cross-references: incident_tickets.json[incident_id], production_logs.json[incident_id].

2. service
   The primary service involved in this event.
   Values: auth | gateway | payments | orders | redis | inventory | notifications | search
   Cross-references: service_dependency_graph.json[nodes[].id], deployment_records.json[service],
   runbooks/[service].json[service].

3. event_type
   The type of event in the incident lifecycle.
   Values:
     ALERT_FIRED         — Automated monitoring alert triggered
     TICKET_OPENED       — Support or engineering ticket created
     INVESTIGATION_START — On-call engineer began active investigation
     ROOT_CAUSE_IDENTIFIED — Root cause determined
     MITIGATION_APPLIED  — A fix or workaround was applied
     DEPLOYMENT          — A code or config change deployed during incident
     IMPACT_DETECTED     — Downstream/upstream service impact observed
     RECOVERY            — Service returned to healthy state
     MONITORING          — Team watching metrics after mitigation
     RESOLVED            — Incident formally marked resolved

4. timestamp
   ISO 8601 UTC timestamp of when this event occurred (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ).
   Cross-references: production_logs.json[timestamp], incident_tickets.json[timestamp],
   deployment_records.json[timestamp].

5. severity
   Incident severity at the time of this event. May downgrade as incident resolves.
   Values: P1 (critical) | P2 (high) | P3 (medium) | P4 (low)
   Cross-references: incident_tickets.json[severity].

6. description
   Human-readable description of what happened at this event step.

7. actor
   Who performed or triggered this action. Can be a person (email), team, or automated system.
   Values: pagerduty | automated-alert | customer-support | on-call-engineer |
           alice@example.com | bob@example.com | carol@example.com | dave@example.com

8. related_ticket_id
   Ticket ID this event is associated with (may be empty if no ticket exists for this event).
   Cross-references: incident_tickets.json[ticket_id].

9. related_deployment_id
   Deployment ID that caused or resolved this incident event (may be empty).
   Cross-references: deployment_records.json[deployment_id].
